narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and an LSAT score of 35, your academic profile stood out in some positive ways, but the law school's admission model focused on predictors of bar exam success using data from thousands of past students. The model estimates a 72.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your application—schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to pass and graduate successfully. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, well below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. This low performance carried the strongest weight because it signals potential challenges in sustaining the rigorous coursework needed for bar prep. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also a bottom-tenth ranking compared to the average around the 6th decile, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often persist. Your LSAT score helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing, as it was close to the dataset average of about 37, showing solid reasoning ability despite not being exceptional. Other details like your upper-middle family income and full-time student status aligned with the majority of applicants and didn't sway the outcome much. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having near-average test scores and background, but those unusually low decile rankings in key undergrad years set you apart in a negative way, outweighing the positives. While disappointing, this isn't a judgment on your potential—many students improve dramatically with targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps through a post-baccalaureate or master's in legal studies to demonstrate growth. Strengthening your LSAT further could also tip future models in your favor. Reach out to admissions advisors for appeal options or alternative pathways like part-time programs, and focus on building a narrative of resilience in your next application. You've got the foundation; refining these key areas can change the story.
